message.content:
Research evaluation and benchmarking frameworks for LLM outputs.

Search the web for:
1. "promptfoo" - LLM evaluation tool
2. "deepeval" - LLM testing
3. "langsmith evaluation"
4. "AI test automation frameworks 2025"

For each tool found, extract:
- Name and URL
- Core concept
- How assertions work
- Integration patterns
- Strengths/weaknesses

Return structured findings focused on how these tools handle assertions and verification.

content.text: Vou pesquisar frameworks de avaliação e benchmarking para outputs de LLM.

Simple declarative configs with command line and CI/CD integration.","url":"https://github.com/promptfoo/promptfoo"},{"title":"Intro | Promptfoo","url":"https://www.promptfoo.dev/docs/intro/"},{"title":"LLM and Prompt Evaluation Frameworks - Prompting - OpenAI Developer Community","url":"https://community.openai.com/t/llm-and-prompt-evaluation-frameworks/945070"},{"title":"From OpenAI to Ollama: Visual LLM Evaluations with Promptfoo - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/ayush7614/from-openai-to-ollama-visual-llm-evaluations-with-promptfoo-2k03"},{"title":"promptfoo: The Open-Source Tool for LLM Evaluation and Red Teaming","url":"https://meterpreter.org/promptfoo-the-open-source-tool-for-llm-evaluation-and-red-teaming/"},{"title":"Promptfoo: LLM Evaluation Tool","url":"https://xaviercollantes.dev/articles/promptfoo"},{"title":"LLM Evaluation Testing with promptfoo: A Practical Guide · The Culture of Code","url":"https://kpavlov.me/blog/llm-evaluation-testing-with-promptfoo-a-practical-guide/"},{"title":"GitHub - AI-App/PromptFoo: Test your prompts. Evaluate and compare LLM outputs, catch regressions, and improve prompt quality.","url":"https://github.com/AI-App/PromptFoo"},{"title":"promptfoo · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/promptfoo"},{"title":"LLM red teaming guide (open source) | Promptfoo","url":"https://www.promptfoo.dev/docs/red-team/"}]

Here's what I found about promptfoo, the LLM evaluation tool:

## Promptfoo Overview

Promptfoo is a developer-friendly local tool for testing LLM applications.(no content)It's an open-source CLI and library for evaluating and red-teaming LLM apps.

### Key Features

Promptfoo allows you to test your prompts, agents, and RAGs. It provides AI red teaming, pentesting, and vulnerability scanning for LLMs. You can compare performance of GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, and more, using simple declarative configs with command line and CI/CD integration.(no content)With promptfoo, you can build reliable prompts, models, and RAGs with benchmarks specific to your use-case, secure your apps with automated red teaming and pentesting, speed up evaluations with caching, concurrency, and live reloading, and score outputs automatically by defining metrics.

### Why Use Promptfoo

Promptfoo is developer friendly and fast, with quality-of-life features like live reloads and caching. It was originally built for LLM apps serving over 10 million users in production.(no content)It offers simple, declarative test cases where you can define evals without writing code or working with heavy notebooks. It's language agnostic (supporting Python, Javascript, or any other language) and has built-in share functionality & web viewer for working with teammates.(no content)The tool is open-source, and this software runs completely locally. The evals run on your machine and talk directly with the LLM.

### Recent 2025 Usage

As of June 2025, promptfoo is being used for practical LLM evaluation testing, including building financial assistant chatbots with Quarkus and LangChain4j.(no content)Promptfoo is described as "your go-to toolkit when you want to test how well your prompts, chat agents, or RAG pipelines hold up under pressure."

### Red Teaming Capabilities

Red teaming provides a quantitative measure of risk before deployment. By running thousands of probes and evaluating the AI's performance, developers can make informed decisions about acceptable risk levels in offline testbeds. Many organizations build this into their development cycle and into processes like CI/CD.

### Getting Started

You can install and initialize a project with `npx promptfoo@latest init` and run your first evaluation with `npx promptfoo eval`.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "langsmith evaluation LLM 2025"

Links: [{"title":"LangSmith Evaluation - Docs by LangChain","url":"https://docs.langchain.com/langsmith/evaluation"},{"title":"LangSmith Evaluation: Tracing & Debugging LLM Apps","url":"https://www.analyticsvidhya.com/blog/2025/11/evaluating-llms-with-langsmith/"},{"title":"Harden your application with LangSmith evaluation","url":"https://www.langchain.com/evaluation"},{"title":"Evaluation","url":"https://www.langchain.com/langsmith/evaluation"},{"title":"Ultimate Langsmith Guide for 2025","url":"https://www.analyticsvidhya.com/blog/2024/07/ultimate-langsmith-guide/"},{"title":"LangWatch vs. LangSmith vs. Braintrust vs. Langfuse: Choosing the Best LLM Evaluation & Monitoring Tool in 2025","url":"https://langwatch.ai/blog/langwatch-vs-langsmith-vs-braintrust-vs-langfuse-choosing-the-best-llm-evaluation-monitoring-tool-in-2025"},{"title":"LangSmith for LangChain: Observability, Tracing & Prompt Evaluation","url":"https://murf.ai/blog/llm-observability-with-langsmith"},{"title":"ADaSci - Global Professional Body of AI Professionals","url":"https://adasci.org/a-practical-guide-to-tracing-and-evaluating-llms-using-langsmith/"},{"title":"The 4 Best LLM Evaluation Platforms in 2025: Why LangWatch redefines the category with Agent Testing (with Simulations)","url":"https://langwatch.ai/blog/the-4-best-llm-evaluation-platforms-in-2025-why-langwatch-eedefines-the-category-with-agent-testing-(with-simulations)"},{"title":"What is LangSmith? | IBM","url":"https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/langsmith"}]

Here are the search results for "langsmith evaluation LLM 2025":

## LangSmith LLM Evaluation Overview

LangSmith is a product created by the LangChain team to address the requirement for great evaluation and debugging tools.(no content)It is an end-to-end set of tools for building, debugging, and deploying LLM-enabled applications, bringing capabilities together into one platform where you can track every step of your LLM app, test output quality, do prompt testing, and deploy management within one environment.

### Key Features

**Evaluation Capabilities:**
- Set up evaluators to run automatically on production traces: safety checks, format validation, quality heuristics, and reference-free LLM-as-judge.
- Use an LLM and prompt to evaluate the response of your application – testing against any custom rubric.
- LangSmith offers both built-in and customizable evaluators to assess performance across various dimensions such as accuracy, relevance and coherence.

**Offline and Online Evaluation:**
- Offline evals run on datasets (great for benchmarking or regression testing). Online evals run on real production traffic in near real time and can be used to monitor deployed agents or LLM apps.

**Dataset Management:**
- A strong testing framework starts with building a reference dataset. LangSmith streamlines this by letting you save debugging and production traces to datasets. Datasets are collections of exemplary or problematic inputs and outputs that should be replicated or corrected, respectively.

### Why LLM Evaluation Matters

Unlike regular software, LLM outputs may differ with the same input because they are probabilistic. This non-determinism makes observability and strict testing important for staying in control of quality.

### Framework Compatibility

LangSmith integrates nicely with LangChain's open-source libraries, but it is not LangChain framework focused only. You can use it even in proprietary LLM pipelines beyond LangChain and other frameworks.

### Enterprise Options

LangSmith allows customers to self-host on the enterprise plan. The software is delivered to run on your Kubernetes cluster, and data will not leave your environment.

### Comparison with Other Tools (2025)

LangSmith, from the creators of LangChain, continues to serve as the go-to evaluation tool for Python-first teams deeply integrated with the LangChain stack. It is ideal for developers already building within the LangChain ecosystem or managing prompt chains with high transparency needs.(no content)Strengths include deep LangChain integration, strong tracing and debugging visualization, mature community support and frequent updates, and solid foundations for chain-level evaluation.(no content)Limitations noted include limited agent simulation or production feedback loops, UI that can overwhelm non-technical collaborators, and secondary JavaScript support. LangSmith is described as an excellent fit for LangChain-native developers, but remains primarily a framework tool, not a full operational evaluation platform.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "deepeval LLM testing framework 2025"

Links: [{"title":"GitHub - confident-ai/deepeval: The LLM Evaluation Framework","url":"https://github.com/confident-ai/deepeval"},{"title":"DeepEval by Confident AI - The LLM Evaluation Framework","url":"https://deepeval.com"},{"title":"Quick Introduction | DeepEval by Confident AI - The LLM Evaluation Framework","url":"https://deepeval.com/docs/getting-started"},{"title":"Evaluate LLMs Effectively Using DeepEval: A Practical Guide | DataCamp","url":"https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/deepeval"},{"title":"Confident AI - The DeepEval LLM Evaluation Platform","url":"https://www.confident-ai.com/"},{"title":"G-Eval | DeepEval by Confident AI - The LLM Evaluation Framework","url":"https://deepeval.com/docs/metrics-llm-evals"},{"title":"Introduction to LLM Metrics | DeepEval by Confident AI - The LLM Evaluation Framework","url":"https://deepeval.com/docs/metrics-introduction"},{"title":"Using DeepEval for Large Language Model (LLM) Evaluation in Python | Codecademy","url":"https://www.codecademy.com/article/using-deepeval-for-llm-evaluation-python"},{"title":"Introduction to LLM Evals | DeepEval - The Open-Source LLM Evaluation Framework","url":"https://deepeval.com/docs/evaluation-introduction"},{"title":"Systematic AI Agent Evaluation: DeepEval Framework Powered by DeepSeek | by Manu Francis | Nov, 2025 | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@manuedavakandam/systematic-ai-agent-evaluation-deepeval-framework-powered-by-deepseek-c81d39b13f8b"}]

Here's what I found about DeepEval, the LLM testing framework:

## DeepEval Overview

DeepEval is a simple-to-use, open-source LLM evaluation framework for evaluating and testing large-language model systems. It is similar to Pytest but specialized for unit testing LLM outputs.

### Key Features

DeepEval incorporates the latest research to evaluate LLM outputs based on metrics such as G-Eval, task completion, answer relevancy, hallucination, etc., which uses LLM-as-a-judge and other NLP models that run locally on your machine for evaluation. Whether your LLM applications are AI agents, RAG pipelines, or chatbots, implemented via LangChain or OpenAI, DeepEval has you covered.(no content)The framework offers plug-and-use 50+ LLM-evaluated metrics, most with research backing, and all are multi-modal. It provides evaluation for RAG, agents, chatbots, and virtually any use case, supporting both end-to-end and component level evaluation. It also includes synthetic dataset generation with state-of-the-art evolution techniques.

### Metrics & Evaluation

Almost all predefined metrics on DeepEval use LLM-as-a-judge, with various techniques such as QAG (question-answer-generation), DAG (deep acyclic graphs), and G-Eval to score test cases. All of DeepEval's metrics output a score between 0-1 based on its corresponding equation, as well as score reasoning. A metric is only successful if the evaluation score is equal to or greater than threshold, which is defaulted to 0.5 for all metrics.(no content)G-Eval is a framework that uses LLM-as-a-judge with chain-of-thoughts (CoT) to evaluate LLM outputs based on ANY custom criteria. The G-Eval metric is the most versatile type of metric DeepEval has to offer, and is capable of evaluating almost any use case with human-like accuracy.

### Red Teaming & Security

You can red team your LLM application for 40+ safety vulnerabilities in a few lines of code.(no content)DeepEval can red team and safety scan LLM applications for security vulnerabilities.

### Integration & CI/CD

DeepEval integrates seamlessly with ANY CI/CD environment.(no content)DeepEval also integrates with model providers including Ollama, Azure OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, etc.

### Cloud Platform

DeepEval has a cloud platform called Confident AI, which allows teams to use DeepEval to evaluate, regression test, red team, and monitor LLM applications on the cloud.

### Cost

DeepEval is completely free and open-source. You only pay for LLM API costs (like OpenAI or Gemini) used as judges for evaluation.

### Requirements

DeepEval works with Python>=3.9+.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"LLM Testing in 2025: Top Methods and Strategies","url":"https://www.confident-ai.com/blog/llm-testing-in-2024-top-methods-and-strategies"},{"title":"12 Best AI Test Automation Tools for 2026: The Third Wave","url":"https://testguild.com/7-innovative-ai-test-automation-tools-future-third-wave/"},{"title":"Mastering Test Automation with LLMs: A Step-by-Step Approach","url":"https://www.frugaltesting.com/blog/mastering-test-automation-with-llms-a-step-by-step-approach"},{"title":"A Review of Open-Source AI-Driven UI Test Automation Frameworks (2025) | by Shubham Sharma | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@ss-tech/a-review-of-open-source-ai-driven-ui-test-automation-frameworks-2025-4b957cdf822d"},{"title":"Top 6 LLM Evaluation Tools to Know in 2025 | Generative AI Collaboration Platform","url":"https://orq.ai/blog/llm-evaluation-tools"},{"title":"RAG-LLM Evals & Test Automation for Beginners | Udemy","url":"https://www.udemy.com/course/rag-llm-evaluation-ai-test/"},{"title":"Modern Test Automation with AI(LLM) and Playwright MCP (Model Context Protocol) | by KailashPathak | Medium","url":"https://kailash-pathak.medium.com/modern-test-automation-with-ai-llm-and-playwright-mcp-model-context-protocol-0c311292c7fb"},{"title":"Revolutionizing software testing: Introducing LLM-powered bug catchers - Engineering at Meta","url":"https://engineering.fb.com/2025/02/05/security/revolutionizing-software-testing-llm-powered-bug-catchers-meta-ach/"},{"title":"How LLMs Are Reshaping QA in 2025","url":"https://cloudqa.io/how-llms-are-reshaping-qa-in-2025/"},{"title":"How LLMs and AI Agents Are Transforming Test Automation | by Saurabh Srivastava | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@saurabh71289/how-llms-and-ai-agents-are-transforming-test-automation-7d0903e0d8c2"}]

Based on the search results, here's an overview of AI test automation frameworks leveraging LLMs in 2025:

## Key LLM Test Automation Frameworks & Tools

### LLM Evaluation & Testing Frameworks

1. **DeepEval** - An open-source LLM evaluation framework built to streamline the benchmarking of LLMs.(no content)It's designed to enforce best testing practices through a variety of robust ready-to-use LLM evaluation metrics, and automated testing in CI/CD pipelines.

2. **RAGAS Framework** - A framework for evaluating RAG-based LLMs, supporting automation of single turn interactions and multi-turn interactions with LLMs, and generating test data for evaluating LLM metrics.

3. **Opik** - An open-source LLM evaluation framework designed for developers and data scientists. It offers a unit-test-style API to create LLM evals and benchmarks, making it a practical choice for teams focused on integrating LLM model evaluation directly into CI workflows.

4. **Orq.ai** - Delivers an end-to-end, full-lifecycle LLM evaluation framework by combining automated and human-in-the-loop evaluation, real-time monitoring, collaborative annotation, and seamless integration with deployment pipelines.

### AI-Driven UI Test Automation

5. **Midscene.js** - An automation tool powered by multimodal AI for UI testing. Users can describe test steps in natural language, with support for English, French, and Chinese.(no content)It supports a range of LLMs including GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5 Pro, Qwen-VL-Max, and UI-TARS, an open-source model specifically designed for UI automation.

6. **Playwright MCP (Model Context Protocol)** - GenAI introduces intelligence into test automation by leveraging LLMs, enabling natural language test creation, self-healing scripts, and dynamic adaptability. The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a standardized interface connecting GenAI's cognitive power with Playwright's automation prowess.

### Enterprise & Specialized Tools

7. **Meta's ACH (Automated Compliance Hardening)** - A system for mutation-guided, LLM-based test generation that hardens platforms against regressions by generating undetected faults (mutants) in source code.(no content)Meta has applied ACH-assisted testing to Facebook Feed, Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp.

8. **Other Open-Source Tools** - Open-source tools like TestZeus, AI Testing Agent, and AutoGen are available for exploring AI-powered automation.(no content)Skyvern combines visual parsing and reasoning to navigate unfamiliar websites, and DroidBot-GPT integrates GPT with Android testing for mobile UIs.

## Key Trends in 2025

- LLMs are reshaping QA by moving up the abstraction ladder. We are no longer testing pixels; we are verifying intent. The tools available in 2025 allow us to test faster, cover more ground, and fix bugs cheaper than ever before.

- Third wave AI testing tools don't just run tests faster—they actively reduce the maintenance burden that's been killing teams since the Selenium days.

- Self-healing capabilities allow AI to analyze page structure when elements change, inferring correct elements through semantic similarity and updating test scripts automatically.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

- Third wave AI testing tools don't just run tests faster—they actively reduce the maintenance burden that's been killing teams since the Selenium days.

- Self-healing capabilities allow AI to analyze page structure when elements change, inferring correct elements through semantic similarity and updating test scripts automatically.

content.content:
# LangSmith Evaluation: Evaluators and Datasets

## How Evaluators Work

LangSmith's evaluation system uses evaluators to score performance on datasets. The platform supports multiple evaluation approaches:

**Reference-based evaluation**: Evaluators compare outputs against expected results from curated datasets during development.

**Reference-free evaluation**: For production monitoring, evaluators run automatically on live traffic without ground truth comparisons.

## Types of Evaluators Available

The documentation identifies four primary evaluator categories:

1. **Human Review** - Manual assessment by team members
2. **Code Rules** - Programmatic scoring logic based on custom criteria
3. **LLM-as-Judge** - AI models evaluating responses autonomously
4. **Pairwise Comparison** - Comparative scoring between multiple outputs

## Custom Evaluator Creation

While the provided content doesn't detail custom creation syntax, it indicates evaluators can be:
- Tailored to specific performance criteria
- Applied to intermediate steps within applications
- Configured with multiple scoring mechanisms
- Bound directly to datasets for repeated testing

## Dataset and Test Structure

Datasets form the foundation of evaluation:

**Dataset composition**: "Create a dataset with examples from manually curated test cases, historical production traces, or synthetic data generation."

**Example organization**: The platform supports structured data formats including JSON schemas for consistent test case formatting.

**Experiment execution**: Applications run against datasets to generate experiments, with configurable options for repetitions, concurrency, and caching optimization.

content.content:
# DeepEval Framework Analysis

## 1) How Assertions Work

DeepEval uses the `assert_test()` function to validate LLM outputs against metrics. The framework accepts a test case and list of metrics, evaluating whether results meet defined thresholds.

"All metric scores range from 0 - 1, which the `threshold=0.5` threshold ultimately determines if your test have passed or not."

The assertion passes when metric scores exceed the specified threshold value.

## 2) Types of Metrics and Evaluators

DeepEval offers **50+ research-backed metrics** covering multiple categories:

- **Custom LLM-based:** GEval, DAG, Conversational G-Eval, Arena G-Eval
- **RAG-focused:** Answer Relevancy and similar retrieval metrics
- **Agent-focused:** Task Completion evaluations
- **Safety:** Bias detection and vulnerability scanning
- **Non-LLM:** Exact Match and similar deterministic metrics
- **Multimodal:** Image coherence and other visual assessments

Most metrics function as "LLM-as-a-Judge" evaluators requiring an API key (OpenAI by default, with Anthropic, Gemini, and other providers supported).

## 3) Test Case Structure

Two primary test case types exist:

**Single-Turn (LLMTestCase):**
- `input`: User query
- `actual_output`: LLM-generated response
- `expected_output`: Ideal answer (optional)

**Multi-Turn (ConversationalTestCase):**
- `turns`: List of Turn objects containing `role` ("user" or "assistant") and `content`
- Evaluates sequential interactions

"An LLM test case in `deepeval` represents a **single unit of LLM app interaction**."

## 4) Integration with Pytest

The documentation doesn't detail direct pytest integration. However, DeepEval uses pytest-compatible patterns: test functions prefixed with `test_`, executable via command-line (`deepeval test run test_example.py`), and boolean pass/fail outcomes based on threshold comparisons.

The framework operates independently rather than as a pytest plugin.

# DeepEval Framework Analysis

## 1) How Assertions Work

DeepEval uses the `assert_test()` function to validate LLM outputs against metrics. The framework accepts a test case and list of metrics, evaluating whether results meet defined thresholds.

"All metric scores range from 0 - 1, which the `threshold=0.5` threshold ultimately determines if your test have passed or not."

The assertion passes when metric scores exceed the specified threshold value.

## 2) Types of Metrics and Evaluators

DeepEval offers **50+ research-backed metrics** covering multiple categories:

- **Custom LLM-based:** GEval, DAG, Conversational G-Eval, Arena G-Eval
- **RAG-focused:** Answer Relevancy and similar retrieval metrics
- **Agent-focused:** Task Completion evaluations
- **Safety:** Bias detection and vulnerability scanning
- **Non-LLM:** Exact Match and similar deterministic metrics
- **Multimodal:** Image coherence and other visual assessments

Most metrics function as "LLM-as-a-Judge" evaluators requiring an API key (OpenAI by default, with Anthropic, Gemini, and other providers supported).

## 3) Test Case Structure

Two primary test case types exist:

**Single-Turn (LLMTestCase):**
- `input`: User query
- `actual_output`: LLM-generated response
- `expected_output`: Ideal answer (optional)

**Multi-Turn (ConversationalTestCase):**
- `turns`: List of Turn objects containing `role` ("user" or "assistant") and `content`
- Evaluates sequential interactions

"An LLM test case in `deepeval` represents a **single unit of LLM app interaction**."

## 4) Integration with Pytest

The documentation doesn't detail direct pytest integration. However, DeepEval uses pytest-compatible patterns: test functions prefixed with `test_`, executable via command-line (`deepeval test run test_example.py`), and boolean pass/fail outcomes based on threshold comparisons.

The framework operates independently rather than as a pytest plugin.

Web search results for query: "deepeval metrics GEval answer relevancy hallucination example"

Web search error: unavailable

I apologize, but the web search tool is currently unavailable. I cannot perform the search for "deepeval metrics GEval answer relevancy hallucination example" at this time.

However, I can share some general knowledge about DeepEval that I have:

**DeepEval** is an open-source evaluation framework for LLM applications. It provides several metrics for evaluating LLM outputs:

1. **G-Eval** - A framework that uses LLMs with chain-of-thought reasoning to evaluate generated text. It allows you to define custom evaluation criteria.

2. **Answer Relevancy** - Measures how relevant the generated answer is to the input question. It ensures the response actually addresses what was asked.

3. **Hallucination** - Detects when the LLM generates information that is not grounded in the provided context or is factually incorrect.

A typical usage example might look like:

```python
from deepeval import evaluate
from deepeval.metrics import AnswerRelevancyMetric, HallucinationMetric, GEval
from deepeval.test_case import LLMTestCase

# Create a test case
test_case = LLMTestCase(
    input="What is the capital of France?",
    actual_output="Paris is the capital of France.",
    context=["France is a country in Europe. Paris is its capital."]
)

# Define metrics
answer_relevancy = AnswerRelevancyMetric(threshold=0.7)
hallucination = HallucinationMetric(threshold=0.5)

# Evaluate
evaluate([test_case], [answer_relevancy, hallucination])
```

If you need the most up-to-date documentation or specific examples, I'd recommend visiting the official DeepEval documentation at docs.confident-ai.com when the search becomes available, or you can try again later.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"Assertions and Metrics - LLM Output Validation | Promptfoo","url":"https://www.promptfoo.dev/docs/configuration/expected-outputs/"},{"title":"Configuration Guide - Getting Started with Promptfoo | Promptfoo","url":"https://www.promptfoo.dev/docs/configuration/guide/"},{"title":"Tests / Evals | GenAIScript","url":"https://microsoft.github.io/genaiscript/reference/scripts/tests/"},{"title":"Deterministic Metrics for LLM Output Validation | Promptfoo","url":"https://www.promptfoo.dev/docs/configuration/expected-outputs/deterministic/"},{"title":"Model-graded metrics | Promptfoo","url":"https://www.promptfoo.dev/docs/configuration/expected-outputs/model-graded/"},{"title":"GitHub - AI-App/PromptFoo: Test your prompts. Evaluate and compare LLM outputs, catch regressions, and improve prompt quality.","url":"https://github.com/AI-App/PromptFoo"},{"title":"Generative AI Evaluation with Promptfoo: A Comprehensive Guide | by Yuki Nagae | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@yukinagae/generative-ai-evaluation-with-promptfoo-a-comprehensive-guide-e23ea95c1bb7"},{"title":"How to Use Promptfoo for LLM Testing","url":"https://stephencollins.tech/posts/how-to-use-promptfoo-for-llm-testing"},{"title":"Configuration Overview - Prompts, Tests, and Outputs | Promptfoo","url":"https://www.promptfoo.dev/docs/configuration/parameters/"},{"title":"LLM Rubric | Promptfoo","url":"https://www.promptfoo.dev/docs/configuration/expected-outputs/model-graded/llm-rubric/"}]

Here's an overview of Promptfoo's assertion types including `contains`, `javascript`, `icontains`, and `llm-rubric`:

## Promptfoo Assertion Types

### Deterministic Assertions

Promptfoo supports many assertion types including:
- **equals** - output matches exactly
- **contains** - output contains substring
- **icontains** - output contains substring, case insensitive
- **regex** - output matches regex
- **starts-with** - output starts with string
- **contains-any** - output contains any of the listed substrings
- **contains-all** - output contains all list of substrings
- **is-json** - output is valid json (optional json schema validation)
- **contains-json** - output contains valid json (optional json schema validation)
- **javascript** - provided Javascript function validates the output
- **python** - provided Python function validates the output(no content)The `contains-any` assertion checks if the LLM output contains at least one of the specified values. For case insensitive matching, use `icontains-any`.(no content)Every test type can be negated by prepending `not-`. For example, `not-equals` or `not-regex`.

### JavaScript Assertions

The javascript assertion runs Javascript against the LLM output. Example usage:

```yaml
assert:
  - type: javascript
    value: output.toLowerCase().includes('bonjour')
```

The value of an assertion can be loaded directly from a file using the `file://` syntax. If the file ends in `.js`, the Javascript is executed.

### LLM-Rubric Assertions

To use the `llm-rubric` assertion type, add it to your test configuration. This assertion will use a language model to grade the output based on the specified rubric. Under the hood, llm-rubric uses a model to evaluate the output based on the criteria you provide.

Example:
```yaml
assert:
  - type: llm-rubric
    value: Is not apologetic and provides a clear, concise answer
```

An llm-rubric assertion uses a Language Model to grade the output against a specific rubric. This method is effective for qualitative assessments of outputs, such as creativity, detail, or adherence to a theme.

### Example Configuration

Here's an example showing multiple assertion types together:
```yaml
tests:
  - vars:
      topic: avocado toast
    assert:
      - type: icontains  # Check that the test result contains the string
        value: avocado
      - type: javascript  # Custom checks with javascript
        value: 1 / (output.length + 1)
  - vars:
      topic: new york city
    assert:
      - type: llm-rubric  # Check output with another generative AI model
        value: ensure that the output is funny
```

### Metrics and Weighting

Each assertion supports a metric field that allows you to tag the result however you like. Use this feature to combine related assertions into aggregate metrics.(no content)The `weight` property is a number that determines the relative importance of the assertion. The default weight is 1. The final score of the test case is calculated as the weighted average of the scores of all assertions.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
# DeepEval Metrics: Comprehensive Overview

## Available Metrics by Category

**Custom Metrics:**
- G-Eval
- DAG (Deep Acyclic Graph)
- Conversational G-Eval
- Conversational DAG
- Arena G-Eval
- Self-coded implementations

**RAG Metrics:**
- Contextual Relevancy, Precision, Recall
- Answer Relevancy
- Faithfulness

**Agentic Metrics:**
- Task Completion
- Argument Correctness
- Tool Correctness
- Step Efficiency
- Plan Adherence
- Plan Quality

**Multi-Turn/Chatbot Metrics:**
- Knowledge Retention
- Role Adherence
- Conversation Completeness
- Conversation Relevancy

**Safety Metrics:**
- Bias, Toxicity
- Non-Advice, Misuse
- PII Leakage
- Role Violation

**Image Metrics:**
- Image Coherence
- Image Helpfulness
- Image Reference
- Text-to-Image
- Image-Editing

**Other Metrics:**
- Hallucination, JSON Correctness
- Summarization, Ragas

## How Metrics Work

Metrics use "LLM-as-a-judge" approaches including QAG, DAG, and G-Eval techniques. They score test cases on a 0-1 scale and provide reasoning for each judgment.

## Threshold Configuration

"A metric is only successful if the evaluation score is equal to or greater than `threshold`, which is defaulted to `0.5` for all metrics." Users can customize thresholds during initialization.

## Code Example: Basic Usage

```python
from deepeval.test_case import LLMTestCase
from deepeval.metrics import AnswerRelevancyMetric

test_case = LLMTestCase(input="...", actual_output="...")
metric = AnswerRelevancyMetric(threshold=0.5)
metric.measure(test_case)
print(metric.score, metric.reason)
```

## Code Example: Custom Metric

```python
from deepeval.metrics import GEval
from deepeval.test_case import LLMTestCaseParams

correctness = GEval(
    name="Correctness",
    criteria="Determine if actual output is correct per expected output.",
    evaluation_params=[LLMTestCaseParams.ACTUAL_OUTPUT],
    strict_mode=True
)
```

## Key Features

- All metrics output scores between 0-1 with reasoning
- Most require only 1-2 parameters
- Async execution available via `a_measure()`
- Customizable prompts through evaluation templates
- Recommended limit: 2-3 generic + 1-2 custom metrics per evaluation

content.content:
# Promptfoo Assertion Types: Complete Reference

## Deterministic Assertions

These programmatic tests validate LLM output without ML models:

| Type | Purpose | Syntax |
|------|---------|--------|
| `equals` | Exact match | `equals:value` |
| `contains` | Substring present | `contains:value` |
| `icontains` | Case-insensitive substring | `icontains:value` |
| `regex` | Pattern matching | `regex:^pattern$` |
| `starts-with` | Beginning match | `starts-with:prefix` |
| `contains-any` | Any substring from list | `contains-any:[list]` |
| `contains-all` | All substrings present | `contains-all:[list]` |
| `is-json` | Valid JSON structure | `is-json` |
| `contains-json` | JSON embedded in output | `contains-json` |
| `is-html` / `contains-html` | HTML validation | `is-html` |
| `is-sql` / `contains-sql` | SQL validation | `is-sql` |
| `is-xml` / `contains-xml` | XML validation | `is-xml` |
| `javascript` | Custom JS logic | `fn:output.length < 100` |
| `python` | Custom Python logic | `python:len(output) > 10` |
| `webhook` | External validation | `webhook` |
| `cost` | Below cost threshold | Cost measured in dollars |
| `latency` | Response speed | Measured in milliseconds |
| `levenshtein` | Edit distance threshold | `levenshtein(5):text` |
| `rouge-n` / `bleu` / `meteor` | NLP scoring | Default thresholds apply |

## Model-Assisted Assertions

These leverage LLMs for evaluation:

| Type | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `similar` | Embedding similarity scoring |
| `llm-rubric` / `grade` | Rubric-based evaluation |
| `g-eval` | Chain-of-thought grading |
| `answer-relevance` | Query alignment |
| `context-faithfulness` | Source fidelity |
| `factuality` | Fact adherence |
| `classifier` | Output classification |
| `select-best` | Compare multiple outputs |

## Key Features

**Negation:** Prepend `not-` to any type (e.g., `not-contains:error`)

**Weighting:** Assign importance via `weight: 2` property

**CSV Format:** Use `__expected` column; multiple assertions via `__expected1`, `__expected2`

**External Files:** Reference with `file://path` syntax for value content

| `is-json` | Valid JSON structure | `is-json` |
| `contains-json` | JSON embedded in output | `contains-json` |
| `is-html` / `contains-html` | HTML validation | `is-html` |
| `is-sql` / `contains-sql` | SQL validation | `is-sql` |
| `is-xml` / `contains-xml` | XML validation | `is-xml` |
| `javascript` | Custom JS logic | `fn:output.length < 100` |
| `python` | Custom Python logic | `python:len(output) > 10` |
| `webhook` | External validation | `webhook` |
| `cost` | Below cost threshold | Cost measured in dollars |
| `latency` | Response speed | Measured in milliseconds |
| `levenshtein` | Edit distance threshold | `levenshtein(5):text` |
| `rouge-n` / `bleu` / `meteor` | NLP scoring | Default thresholds apply |

## Model-Assisted Assertions

These leverage LLMs for evaluation:

| Type | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `similar` | Embedding similarity scoring |
| `llm-rubric` / `grade` | Rubric-based evaluation |
| `g-eval` | Chain-of-thought grading |
| `answer-relevance` | Query alignment |
| `context-faithfulness` | Source fidelity |
| `factuality` | Fact adherence |
| `classifier` | Output classification |
| `select-best` | Compare multiple outputs |

## Key Features

**Negation:** Prepend `not-` to any type (e.g., `not-contains:error`)

**Weighting:** Assign importance via `weight: 2` property

**CSV Format:** Use `__expected` column; multiple assertions via `__expected1`, `__expected2`

**External Files:** Reference with `file://path` syntax for value content

Web search results for query: "RAGAS framework RAG evaluation metrics 2025"

Web search error: unavailable

I apologize, but the web search tool is currently unavailable. I cannot perform the search for "RAGAS framework RAG evaluation metrics 2025" at this time.

However, I can share some general information about RAGAS (Retrieval Augmented Generation Assessment) based on my existing knowledge:

**RAGAS** is an open-source framework designed to evaluate RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) pipelines. It provides several key metrics:

1. **Faithfulness** - Measures how factually accurate the generated answer is based on the retrieved context
2. **Answer Relevancy** - Evaluates how relevant the generated answer is to the question
3. **Context Precision** - Assesses whether the relevant items in the context are ranked higher
4. **Context Recall** - Measures if all relevant information needed to answer the question was retrieved
5. **Context Relevancy** - Evaluates how relevant the retrieved context is to the question

If you need the most up-to-date information about RAGAS in 2025, I'd recommend:
- Checking the official RAGAS GitHub repository
- Visiting the RAGAS documentation at docs.ragas.io
- Looking at recent blog posts or papers about RAG evaluation

Would you like me to try the search again later, or is there anything else I can help you with regarding RAG evaluation?

Here's what I found about Opik, the open-source LLM evaluation framework:

## Opik by Comet

Opik is an open-source end-to-end LLM evaluation platform developed by Comet, a leading platform for experimentation and model production management.(no content)It is designed to assess Large Language Models (LLMs) and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems.

### Key Features

Opik is a true open-source project, and its full LLM evaluation feature set is included free in the source code. Users can download the code from GitHub and run it locally, with a highly scalable and industry-compliant version ready for enterprise use.

**Tracing & Debugging:**
Opik enables developers to log and debug traces and spans across even the most intricate workflows. This visibility allows teams to identify bottlenecks and errors swiftly, enhancing overall system reliability.

**Evaluation Capabilities:**
- Opik simplifies evaluation by allowing users to implement both heuristic and LLM-based evaluation judges with minimal code. This flexibility empowers teams to customize their evaluation criteria based on project requirements.
- The Python Opik SDK includes a number of LLM as a judge metrics to help you evaluate your LLM application.
- Consult built-in LLM judges for complex issues like hallucination detection, factuality, and moderation.

**CI/CD Integration:**
Comet's Opik supports the creation of "model unit tests" using Pytest, enabling developers to integrate these tests into their CI/CD pipelines. This approach automates evaluation checks during development cycles, reducing the risk of deploying faulty models.

**Production Monitoring:**
